title: International Business Machines (IBM) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: Opus 4.6 independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of International Business Machines (IBM). Economic Prospect Score: 57/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y10)
fcfGrowth.rationale: International Business Machines is projected to grow FCF at 5% annually, reflecting its competitive position, sector dynamics, and historical compounding rate.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% WACC balances International Business Machines's earnings quality and balance sheet strength against sector volatility and macroeconomic risk factors.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.5% terminal rate assumes long-run growth converges toward nominal GDP, appropriate for a mature S&P 500 constituent.

thesisTitle: International Business Machines: A Fundamental Assessment
thesisParagraphs: International Business Machines (IBM) is a large-cap constituent of the S&P 500 with established market position and consistent cash flow generation. The company's competitive advantages and market share provide a degree of pricing power and earnings stability that supports a premium valuation.

My DCF applies a 5% FCF growth rate reflecting the company's historical compounding ability and forward outlook, with a 9.0% discount rate capturing sector-specific risks and capital market conditions. The valuation is sensitive to assumptions about sustainable growth — investors should stress-test these inputs against their own convictions.
verdictFooter: Opus 4.6 independent analysis. Data via public filings (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is International Business Machines's intrinsic value according to Opus?
faq.answer: Based on a 10-year DCF with 5% FCF growth and 9.0% WACC, Opus calculates IBM's intrinsic value at approximately $245.66 per share, suggesting the stock is fairly valued at $250.00.

faq.question: What assumptions drive IBM's valuation?
faq.answer: The key drivers are the FCF growth rate (5%) and discount rate (9.0%). A 1% increase in growth adds roughly 10-15% to the intrinsic value. These assumptions reflect Opus's independent assessment of the company's competitive position and risk profile.

faq.question: Should I buy or sell based on this analysis?
faq.answer: This is not financial advice. DCF models are inherently subjective — small changes in assumptions create large swings in output. Use this as one data point among many in your investment research process.

prospectVerdictDetail: IBM's Red Hat acquisition and hybrid cloud strategy are driving stable growth. WatsonX AI platform provides relevance in the AI wave. But growth is modest, the consulting business is cyclical, and the stock has destroyed shareholder value for over a decade.
prospectKeyRisks: Consulting revenue declining as enterprises rationalize IT spending
WatsonX failing to gain meaningful traction against Microsoft/Google AI platforms
Mainframe revenue declining as workloads migrate to cloud
prospectKeyCatalysts: Red Hat OpenShift becoming the default hybrid cloud platform for regulated industries
WatsonX adoption in enterprise AI governance and compliance use cases
Mainframe modernization services driving consulting revenue as customers upgrade rather than migrate
prospectMethodology: Score is based on three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30), totaling 0-100. Each pillar is broken into individually scored factors with transparent rationale. Data sources include FY2025 10-K filings, analyst consensus estimates, news sentiment analysis, and competitive landscape assessment. The score is forward-looking and represents economic prospect over a 2-3 year horizon.
